subject: Mathematics
q: If in a triangle $\mathrm{ABC} \mathrm{a} \cos ^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}\right)+\mathrm{c}^2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)=\frac{3 \mathrm{~b}}{2}$, then the sides $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ and $\mathrm{c}$
question: If in a triangle $\mathrm{ABC} \mathrm{a} \cos ^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}\right)+\mathrm{c}^2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)=\frac{3 \mathrm{~b}}{2}$, then the sides $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ and $\mathrm{c}$
solution:
If $a \cos ^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}\right)+\cos ^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)=\frac{3 \mathrm{~b}}{2}$
$\mathrm{a}[\cos \mathrm{C}+1]+\mathrm{c}[\cos \mathrm{A}+1]=3 \mathrm{~b}$
$(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c})+(\mathrm{a} \cos \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{c} \cos \mathrm{B})=3 \mathrm{~b}$
$\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{b}=3 \mathrm{~b}$ or $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}=2 \mathrm{~b}$ or $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are in A.P.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> be the length of sides of a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow><mn>7</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow><mn>8</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>c</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow><mn>9</mn></mfrac></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are the radius of incircle and radius of circumcircle of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, respectively, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mi>r</mi></mfrac></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> be the length of sides of a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow><mn>7</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow><mn>8</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>c</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow><mn>9</mn></mfrac></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are the radius of incircle and radius of circumcircle of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, respectively, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mi>r</mi></mfrac></math> is equal to
solution:
Let a+b7=b+c8=c+a9=k

so a+b=7k, b+c=8k, c+a=9k

Solving this we get a=4k, b=3k, c=5k and calculating semi-perimeter 

S=a+b+c2 ⇒S=6k

We know that rR=4sinA2sinB2sinC2

⇒  rR=4s-bs-cbc×s-as-cac×s-as-bab

⇒rR=4·s-as-bs-cabc

⇒rR=4·2k3kk4k3k5k ⇒Rr=52

subject: Mathematics
q: In a triangle $\mathrm{PQR}, \angle \mathrm{R}=\frac{\pi}{2}$. If $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right)$ and $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)$ are the roots of $a x^2+b x+c=0, a \neq 0$ then
question: In a triangle $\mathrm{PQR}, \angle \mathrm{R}=\frac{\pi}{2}$. If $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right)$ and $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)$ are the roots of $a x^2+b x+c=0, a \neq 0$ then
solution:
$\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right), \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)$ are the roots of $\mathrm{ax}^2+\mathrm{bx}+\mathrm{c}=0$
$$
\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right)+\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)=-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}
$$
$\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right) \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{a}}$
$\frac{\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right)+\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)}{1-\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right) \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)}=\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)=1$
$\Rightarrow \frac{-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}}{1-\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{a}}}=1 \Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}}-\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{a}} \Rightarrow-\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{c}$
$\mathrm{c}=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}$.

subject: Mathematics
q: If two vertices of an equilateral triangle are $\mathrm{A}(-a, 0)$ and $B(a, 0), a>0$, and the third vertex C lies above $x$-axis then the equation of the circumcircle of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is :
question: If two vertices of an equilateral triangle are $\mathrm{A}(-a, 0)$ and $B(a, 0), a>0$, and the third vertex C lies above $x$-axis then the equation of the circumcircle of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is :
solution:
Let $\mathrm{C}=(x, y)$
Now, $\mathrm{CA}^2=\mathrm{CB}^2=\mathrm{AB}^2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow(x+a)^2+y^2=(x-a)^2+y^2=(2 a)^2 \\
& \Rightarrow x^2+2 a x+a^2+y^2=4 a^2 \\
& \text { and } x^2-2 a x+a^2+y^2=4 a^2
\end{aligned}
$$
From (i) and (ii), $x=0$ and $y=\pm \sqrt{3} a$
Since point $\mathrm{C}(x, y)$ lies above the $x$-axis and $a>0$, hence $y=\sqrt{3} a$
$$
\therefore \quad \mathrm{C}=(0, \sqrt{3} a)
$$
Let the equation of circumcircle be
$$
x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+\mathrm{C}=0
$$
Since points A( $-a, 0), \mathrm{B}(a, 0)$ and $\mathrm{C}(0, \sqrt{3} a)$ lie on the circle, therefore
$$
\begin{aligned}
& a^2-2 g a+\mathrm{C}=0 \\
& a^2+2 g a+\mathrm{C}=0
\end{aligned}
$$
and $3 a^2+2 \sqrt{3} a f+\mathrm{C}=0$
From (iii), (iv), and (v)
$$
g=0, c=-a^2, f=-\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}
$$
Hence equation of the circumcircle is
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x^2+y^2-\frac{2 a}{\sqrt{3}} y-a^2=0 \\
& \Rightarrow x^2+y^2-\frac{2 \sqrt{3} a y}{3}-a^2=0 \\
& \Rightarrow 3 x^2+3 y^2-2 \sqrt{3} a y=3 a^2
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: The equation of a circle with origin as a centre and passing through equilateral triangle whose median is of length 3a is
question: The equation of a circle with origin as a centre and passing through equilateral triangle whose median is of length 3a is
solution: Let $A B C$ be an equilateral triangle, whose median is $A D$. Given $A D=3 a$ In $\triangle \mathrm{ABD}, \mathrm{AB}^2=\mathrm{AD}^2+\mathrm{BD}^2$ $\Rightarrow x^2=9 a^2+\left(x^2 / 4\right)$ where $A B=B C=A C=x . \frac{3}{4} x^2=9 a^2 \Rightarrow x^2=12 a^2$ In $\triangle \mathrm{OBD}, \mathrm{OB}^2=\mathrm{OD}^2+\mathrm{BD}^2$ $\Rightarrow r^2=(3 a-r)^2+\frac{x^2}{4} \Rightarrow r^2=9 a^2-6 a r+r^2+3 a^2 ; \Rightarrow 6 a r=12 a^2 \Rightarrow r=2 a$ So equation of circle is $x^2+y^2=4 a^2$

subject: Mathematics
q: The angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi>C</mi></math> of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> are in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the area (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>s</mi><mi>q</mi><mo>.</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math>) of this triangle is:
question: The angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi>C</mi></math> of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> are in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the area (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>s</mi><mi>q</mi><mo>.</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math>) of this triangle is:
solution:
In ∆ ABC, angles A, B, C are in A.P. hence 2∠B=∠A+∠C

Also, by angle sum property ∠A+∠B+∠C=180°

⇒2∠B+∠B=180°

⇒∠B=60°.

Now a:b=1:3

By, using sine rule, we have sinAa=sinBb=sinCc

⇒sinAa=sinBb

⇒sinAsin60°=ab

⇒sinA=13 ·3 2=12

⇒∠A=30° and hence ∠C=90°

Thus, the triangle ABC is a right triangle, with hypotenuse AB=4 cm.

Again, by Sine rule, we have sin30°a=sin60°b=sin90°4

⇒12a=32b=14

⇒12a=14 and 32b=14

⇒a=2 cm and b=23 cm

Now, the area of ∆ABC=12base×height

=12a·b

=12×2×23=23 sq. cm.

subject: Mathematics
q: The sides of a triangle are $\sin \alpha, \cos \alpha$ and $\sqrt{1+\sin \alpha \cos \alpha}$ for some $0 < \alpha < \frac{\pi}{2}$. Then the greatest angle of the triangle is
question: The sides of a triangle are $\sin \alpha, \cos \alpha$ and $\sqrt{1+\sin \alpha \cos \alpha}$ for some $0 < \alpha < \frac{\pi}{2}$. Then the greatest angle of the triangle is
solution: Greatest side is $\sqrt{1+\sin \alpha \cos \alpha}$, by applying cos rule we get greatest angle $=120^{\circ}$.

subject: Mathematics
q: In a triangle with sides $a, b, c, r_1>r_2>r_3$ (which are the ex-radii) then
question: In a triangle with sides $a, b, c, r_1>r_2>r_3$ (which are the ex-radii) then
solution: $r_1>r_2>r_3 \Rightarrow \frac{\Delta}{s-a}>\frac{\Delta}{s-b}>\frac{\Delta}{s-c} \Rightarrow s-a < s-b < s-c \Rightarrow-a < -b < -c \Rightarrow a>b>c$

subject: Mathematics
q: In a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}, \mathrm{BC}=7, \mathrm{AC}=8, \mathrm{AB}=\alpha \in \mathrm{N}$ and $\cos \mathrm{A}=\frac{2}{3}$. If $49 \cos (3 \mathrm{C})+42=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}}$, where $\operatorname{gcd}(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1$, then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to________
question: In a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}, \mathrm{BC}=7, \mathrm{AC}=8, \mathrm{AB}=\alpha \in \mathrm{N}$ and $\cos \mathrm{A}=\frac{2}{3}$. If $49 \cos (3 \mathrm{C})+42=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}}$, where $\operatorname{gcd}(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1$, then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \cos \mathrm{A}=\frac{\mathrm{b}^2+\mathrm{c}^2-\mathrm{a}^2}{2 \mathrm{bc}} \\ & \frac{2}{3}=\frac{8^2+\mathrm{c}^2-7^2}{2 \times 8 \times \mathrm{c}} \\ & \mathrm{C}=9 \\ & \cos \mathrm{C}=\frac{7^2+8^2-9^2}{2 \times 7 \times 8}=\frac{2}{7} \\ & 49 \cos 3 \mathrm{C}+42 \\ & 49\left(4 \cos ^3 \mathrm{C}-3 \cos \mathrm{C}\right)+42 \\ & 49\left(4\left(\frac{2}{7}\right)^3-3\left(\frac{2}{7}\right)\right)+42 \\ & =\frac{32}{7} \\ & \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{n}=32+7=39\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: A triangular park is enclosed on two sides by a fence and on the third side by a straight river bank. The two sides having fence are of same length x. The maximum area enclosed by the park is
question: A triangular park is enclosed on two sides by a fence and on the third side by a straight river bank. The two sides having fence are of same length x. The maximum area enclosed by the park is
solution: Area $=\frac{1}{2} x^2 \sin \theta$ $A_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} x^2\left(\right.$ at $\left.\sin \theta=1, \quad \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$

subject: Mathematics
q: For a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>A</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>B</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>C</mi></math> is least. If its inradius is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> and incentre is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>, then which of the following is NOT correct?
question: For a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>A</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>B</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>C</mi></math> is least. If its inradius is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> and incentre is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>, then which of the following is NOT correct?
solution:
We know that,

cos2A+cos2B+cos2C≥-32

i.e., mincos2A+cos2B+cos2C=-32

So,

cos2A=cos2B=cos2C=-12

⇒A=B=C=60°

So, triangle ABC is an equilateral triangle.

Let the side of triangle be a.

Also,

r=∆s=3

⇒2∆2s=3

⇒234a23a=3

⇒a=183=63

So, perimeter is =3a=183 units.

Area=34a2=813=273 sq. units

subject: Mathematics
q: In a $\Delta \mathrm{PQR}$, if $3 \sin \mathrm{P}+4 \cos \mathrm{Q}=6$ and $4 \sin \mathrm{Q}+3 \cos \mathrm{P}=1$, then the angle $\mathrm{R}$ is equal to
question: In a $\Delta \mathrm{PQR}$, if $3 \sin \mathrm{P}+4 \cos \mathrm{Q}=6$ and $4 \sin \mathrm{Q}+3 \cos \mathrm{P}=1$, then the angle $\mathrm{R}$ is equal to
solution:
$3 \sin P+4 \cos Q=6 \quad\dots(1)$
$4 \sin Q+3 \cos P=1\quad\dots(2)$
From $(1)$ and $(2) \angle P$ is obtuse.
$(3 \sin P+4 \cos Q)^2+(4 \sin Q+3 \cos P)^2=37$
$\Rightarrow 9+16+24(\sin P \cos Q+\cos P \sin Q)=37$
$\Rightarrow 24 \sin (P+Q)=12$
$\Rightarrow \sin (P+Q)=\frac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow P+Q=\frac{5 \pi}{6} \quad \Rightarrow R=\frac{\pi}{6}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>A</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>C</mi></math> are angles of a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the lengths of the sides opposite these angles are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi></math> respectively, then
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>A</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>C</mi></math> are angles of a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the lengths of the sides opposite these angles are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi></math> respectively, then
solution:
Let sin Aa=sin Bb=sin Cc=k⇒sin A=ak, sin B =bk & sin C=ck

As A+B+C=π

⇒B+C=π-A , ⇒sinB+C=sinπ-A=sin A

Similarly, sinA+C=sin B

⇒sinB+CsinA+C=sinA-CsinC-B

⇒sinB+CsinC-B=sinA+CsinA-C

⇒sin2C-sin2B=sin2A-sin2C

⇒c2-b2=a2-c2 ⇒2c2=a2+b2

therefore we can say that b2,c2,a2 are in A.P.

subject: Mathematics
q: On the sides $\mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{CA}$ of a $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}, 3,4,5$ distinct points (excluding vertices $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ ) are respectively chosen. The number of triangles that can be constructed using these chosen points as vertices are :
question: On the sides $\mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{CA}$ of a $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}, 3,4,5$ distinct points (excluding vertices $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ ) are respectively chosen. The number of triangles that can be constructed using these chosen points as vertices are :
solution:
Required number of triangles
$$
={ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_3-\left({ }^3 \mathrm{C}_3+{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_3+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3\right)=205
$$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Given $\frac{\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}}{11}=\frac{\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}}{12}=\frac{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}}{13}$ for a $\Delta \mathrm{ABC}$ with usual

notation. If $\frac{\cos \mathrm{A}}{\alpha}=\frac{\cos \mathrm{B}}{\beta}=\frac{\cos \mathrm{C}}{\gamma},$ then the ordered triad$(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ has a value
question:
Given $\frac{\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}}{11}=\frac{\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}}{12}=\frac{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}}{13}$ for a $\Delta \mathrm{ABC}$ with usual

notation. If $\frac{\cos \mathrm{A}}{\alpha}=\frac{\cos \mathrm{B}}{\beta}=\frac{\cos \mathrm{C}}{\gamma},$ then the ordered triad$(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ has a value
solution:
Let $\frac{b+c}{11}=\frac{c+a}{12}=\frac{a+b}{13}=k$ (Say).

$\therefore \quad b+c=11 k, c+a=12 k, a+b=13 k$

$\therefore \quad a+b+c=18 k$

$\therefore \quad a=7 k, b=6 k$ and $c=5 k$

$\therefore \quad \cos A=\frac{36 k^{2}+25 k^{2}-49 k^{2}}{2.30 k^{2}}=\frac{1}{5}$

and $\cos B=\frac{49 k^{2}+25 k^{2}-36 k^{2}}{2.35 k^{2}}=\frac{19}{35}$

and $\cos C=\frac{49 k^{2}+36 k^{2}-25 k^{2}}{2.42 k^{2}}=\frac{5}{7}$

$\therefore \quad \cos A: \cos B: \cos C=7: 19: 25$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\cos A}{7}=\frac{\cos B}{19}=\frac{\cos C}{25}$

Hence, required ordered triplet is (7,19,25)

subject: Mathematics
q: With the usual notation in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>∠</mi><mi>A</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>∠</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>120</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math> units and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> units, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>∠</mi><mi>A</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>∠</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is
question: With the usual notation in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>∠</mi><mi>A</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>∠</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>120</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math> units and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> units, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>∠</mi><mi>A</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>∠</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is
solution:
Let angle B is x.

Using sine rule, we get

3+1sin120°-x=3-1sinx.

⇒3+13-1=sin120°-xsinx

⇒3+13-1=sin120°cosx-cos120°sinxsinx

⇒3+13-1=sin120°cotx-cos120°

⇒3+13-1-12=32cotx

⇒3+232=32cotx

⇒cotx=3+2

⇒tanx=2-3

⇒x=∠B=15°

⇒120-x=∠A=105°

⇒∠A:∠B=7:1

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the orthocentre and centroid of a triangle be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is the circumcentre of this triangle, then the radius of the circle having line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> as diameter, is:
question: Let the orthocentre and centroid of a triangle be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is the circumcentre of this triangle, then the radius of the circle having line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> as diameter, is:
solution:
Centroid B divides the line joining Orthocentre A and circumcentre C in the ratio 2:1

AB= 3+32+5-32= 62+22= 40=210

AC=32×210=310

Radius =AC2=3104=352

subject: Mathematics
q: If the lengths of the sides of a triangle are in A.P and the greatest angle is double the smallest, then a ratio of lengths of the sides of this triangle is:
question: If the lengths of the sides of a triangle are in A.P and the greatest angle is double the smallest, then a ratio of lengths of the sides of this triangle is:
solution:
Let a<b<c are three sides and θ is the smallest angle. Hence three angles are θ, π-3θ, 2θ
Given, 2b=a+c⇒2sin⁡B=sin⁡A+sin⁡C (using sine rule)
⇒2sin 3θ=sin θ+sin 2θ
⇒23sin θ-4sin3θ=sin θ1+2cos θ
⇒6-81-cos2θ=1+2cos θ
⇒cos θ=34, -12 ( -12 is rejected since θ is acute)
∴ a:b:c=sinA:sin B:sin C=sin θ:sin 3θ:  sin 2θ
=1:3-4 sin2θ:2cos θ=1:4cos2θ-1:2 cos θ
=4:5:6

subject: Mathematics
q: In a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, coordinates of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the equations of the medians through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> are respectively, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. Then area of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> (in sq. units) is :
question: In a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, coordinates of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the equations of the medians through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> are respectively, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. Then area of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> (in sq. units) is :
solution:
Let C4, α be on x=4.

Now, midpoint of AC is 52, α+22 which lies on x+y=5.

∴α+22=52⇒α=3

∴C4, 3

Similarly, let Bβ, 5-β be on x+y=5.

The midpoint of AB =β+12, 7-β2 which lies on x=4⇒β=7⇒B7, -2.

   

∴ Area of Δ=121217-21431

=121-2-3-27-4+121+8=1218=9 sq. units

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\mathrm{A}(2,3,5), \mathrm{B}(-1,3,2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(\lambda, 5, \mu)$ be the vertices of a $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$. If the median through $\mathrm{A}$ is equally inclined to the coordinate axes, then:
question: Let $\mathrm{A}(2,3,5), \mathrm{B}(-1,3,2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(\lambda, 5, \mu)$ be the vertices of a $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$. If the median through $\mathrm{A}$ is equally inclined to the coordinate axes, then:
solution:
If D be the mid-point of $B C$, then $$ D=\left(\frac{\lambda-1}{2}, 4, \frac{\mu+2}{2}\right) $$
Direction ratios of $\mathrm{AD}$ are $\frac{\lambda-5}{2}, 1, \frac{\mu-8}{2}$ Since median AD is equally inclined with coordinate axes, therefore direction ratios of $\mathrm{AD}$ will be equal, i.e, $$ \begin{aligned} &\frac{\left(\frac{\lambda-5}{2}\right)^2}{\left(\frac{\lambda-5}{2}\right)^2+1+\left(\frac{\mu-8}{2}\right)^2} \\ &=\frac{1}{\left(\frac{\lambda-5}{2}\right)^2+1+\left(\frac{\mu-8}{2}\right)^2} \\ &=\frac{\left(\frac{\mu-8}{2}\right)^2}{\left(\frac{\lambda-5}{2}\right)^2+1+\left(\frac{\mu-8}{2}\right)^2} \\ &\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\lambda-5}{2}\right)^2=1=\left(\frac{\mu-8}{2}\right)^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\lambda-5}{2}\right)^2=1=\left(\frac{\mu-8}{2}\right)^2 \\ &\Rightarrow \lambda=7,3 \text { and } \mu=10,6 \end{aligned} $$ If $\lambda=7$ and $\mu=10$ Then $\frac{\lambda}{\mu}=\frac{7}{10} \Rightarrow 10 \lambda-7 \mu=0$

subject: Mathematics
q: If in a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> units, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> and radius of circumcircle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> units, then the area (in sq. units) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> is:
question: If in a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> units, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> and radius of circumcircle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> units, then the area (in sq. units) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> is:
solution:
We have,

As,

cosB=35⇒sinB=45

Given:

R=5

By sine rule, we have

csinC=2R

⇒510=sinC

⇒C=30°
Now,

bsinB=2R

⇒b=45×10=8

Now, by cosine formula

cosB=a2+c2-b22ac

⇒35=a2+25-6425a

⇒a2-6a-39=0

⇒a=6±1922=6±832

⇒a=3+43 

(Reject a=3-43<0)

Now, Δ=abc4R=3+43·8·54·5=23+43

⇒Δ=6+83 sq. units.

question: If in a triangle $A B C, \frac{b+c}{11}=\frac{c+a}{12}=\frac{a+b}{13}$, then $\cos \mathrm{A}$ is equal to

subject: Mathematics
q: In a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$, medians $\mathrm{AD}$ and $\mathrm{BE}$ are drawn. If $\mathrm{AD}=4, \angle \mathrm{DAB}=\frac{\pi}{6}$ and $\angle \mathrm{ABE}=\frac{\pi}{3}$, then the area of the $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is
question: In a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$, medians $\mathrm{AD}$ and $\mathrm{BE}$ are drawn. If $\mathrm{AD}=4, \angle \mathrm{DAB}=\frac{\pi}{6}$ and $\angle \mathrm{ABE}=\frac{\pi}{3}$, then the area of the $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is
solution:
$\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{8 / 3}{x}$ or $x=\frac{8}{3 \sqrt{3}}$
Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABD}=\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times \frac{8}{3 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{16}{3 \sqrt{3}}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the extremities of the base of an isosceles triangle are the points $(2 a, 0)$ and $(0, a)$ and the equation of one of the sides is $x=2 a$, then the area of the triangle, in square units, is :
question: If the extremities of the base of an isosceles triangle are the points $(2 a, 0)$ and $(0, a)$ and the equation of one of the sides is $x=2 a$, then the area of the triangle, in square units, is :
solution:
$$ \text { Let } y \text {-coordinate of } \mathrm{C}=b $$
$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{AB}=\sqrt{4 a^2+a^2}=\sqrt{5} a \\ & \text { Now, } \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{BC} \Rightarrow b=\sqrt{4 a^2+(b-a)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow b^2=4 a^2+b^2+a^2-2 a b \\ & \Rightarrow 2 a b=5 a^2 \Rightarrow b=\frac{5 a}{2} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{C}=\left(2 a, \frac{5 a}{2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Hence area of the triangle $$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 a & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & a & 1 \\ 2 a & \frac{5 a}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right|=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 a & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & a & 1 \\ 0 & \frac{5 a}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 2 a\left(-\frac{5 a}{2}\right)=-\frac{5 a^2}{2} \end{aligned} $$ Since area is always $+\mathrm{ve}$, hence area $$ =\frac{5 a^2}{2} \text { sq. unit } $$

subject: Mathematics
q: ABC is a triangle. Forces $\vec{P}, \vec{Q}, \vec{R}$ acting along $I A, I B$ and $I C$ respectively are in equilibrium, where $I$ is the incentre of $\triangle A B C$. Then $P: Q: R$ is
question: ABC is a triangle. Forces $\vec{P}, \vec{Q}, \vec{R}$ acting along $I A, I B$ and $I C$ respectively are in equilibrium, where $I$ is the incentre of $\triangle A B C$. Then $P: Q: R$ is
solution: Using Lami's Theorem $$ \therefore P: Q: R=\cos \frac{A}{2}: \cos \frac{B}{2}: \cos \frac{C}{2} \text {. } $$

subject: Mathematics
q: In a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$, let $\angle \mathrm{C}=\frac{\pi}{2}$. If $\mathrm{r}$ is the inradius and $\mathrm{R}$ is the circumradius of the the triangle $A B C$, then $2(r+R)$ equals
question: In a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$, let $\angle \mathrm{C}=\frac{\pi}{2}$. If $\mathrm{r}$ is the inradius and $\mathrm{R}$ is the circumradius of the the triangle $A B C$, then $2(r+R)$ equals
solution:
$$
2 r+2 R=c+\frac{2 a b}{(a+b+c)}=\frac{(a+b)^2+c(a+b)}{(a+b+c)}=a+b \quad\left(\text { since } c^2=a^2+b^2\right)
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: The sum of the radii of inscribed and circumscribed circles for an $\mathrm{n}$ sided regular polygon of side a, is
question: The sum of the radii of inscribed and circumscribed circles for an $\mathrm{n}$ sided regular polygon of side a, is
solution:
$\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{n}\right)=\frac{a}{2 r} ; \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{n}\right)=\frac{a}{2 R}$

$r+R=\frac{a}{2}\left[\cot \frac{\pi}{n}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{\pi}{n}\right] \Rightarrow r+R=\frac{a}{2} \cdot \cot \left(\frac{\pi}{2 n}\right)$

subject: Mathematics
q: For a regular polygon, let $\mathrm{r}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ be the radii of the inscribed and the circumscribed circles. A false statement among the following is
question: For a regular polygon, let $\mathrm{r}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ be the radii of the inscribed and the circumscribed circles. A false statement among the following is
options: There is a regular polygon with $\frac{r}{R}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
There is a regular polygon with $\frac{r}{R}=\frac{2}{3}$
There is a regular polygon with $\frac{r}{R}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
There is a regular polygon with $\frac{r}{R}=\frac{1}{2}$
solution:
$$
\mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2} \cot \frac{\pi}{\mathrm{n}}
$$
'a' is side of polygon.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& R=\frac{a}{2} \operatorname{cosec} \frac{\pi}{n} \\
& \frac{r}{R}=\frac{\cot \frac{\pi}{n}}{\operatorname{cosec} \frac{\pi}{n}}=\cos \frac{\pi}{n} \\
& \cos \frac{\pi}{n} \neq \frac{2}{3} \text { for any } n \in N
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let in a right angled triangle, the smallest angle be θ. If a triangle formed by taking the reciprocal of its sides is also a right angled triangle, then sinθ is equal to:
question: Let in a right angled triangle, the smallest angle be θ. If a triangle formed by taking the reciprocal of its sides is also a right angled triangle, then sinθ is equal to:
solution:
Let A=θ, then B=90°-θ.

Hence, a is the smallest side.

Then, in ∆ACB

c2=a2+b2

On taking reciprocal, longest side become shortest and vice versa.

⇒1a2=1b2+1c2

⇒b2c2a2=b2+c2  ...i

Use a=2RsinA=2Rsinθ

b=2RsinB=2Rsin(90°-θ)=2Rcosθ

c=2RsinC=2sin90°=2R

Hence, by i

4R2cos2θ·4R24R2sin2θ=4R2cos2θ+4R2

⇒cos2θ=sin2θcos2θ+sin2θ

⇒1-sin2θ=sin2θ1-sin2θ+sin2θ

⇒1=3sin2θ-sin4θ

⇒sin4θ-3sin2θ+1=0

⇒sin2θ=3-52=5+1-254=5-122

⇒sinθ=5-12

subject: Mathematics
q: In <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ΔABC</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the lengths of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. If the area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>ABC</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively the radii of circumcircle and incircle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ΔABC</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>r</mi><mo> </mo><mo>(</mo><mtext>in cm</mtext><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to ______ .
question: In <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ΔABC</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the lengths of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. If the area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>ABC</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively the radii of circumcircle and incircle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ΔABC</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>r</mi><mo> </mo><mo>(</mo><mtext>in cm</mtext><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to ______ .
solution:
Δ=12·5·12·sinA=30

sinA=1

A=90°⇒BC=13

BC=2R=13

r=ΔS=3015=2

2R+r=15
